name: Texas Muslim Womens Foundation Inc
mission: Founded in 2005, Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) aims to empower, promote and support all women and their families, with a special focus on North Texas. We are an equal opportunity provider. We engage all members of the community – women, men, seniors, families and youth through our various programs. We provide social and family violence services that foster peace and well-being in the home through our Peace in the Home Services and Peaceful Oasis Shelter. In addition, TMWF also arranges leadership skills development for Muslim youth to inspire the next generation to build a promising future for our society and educational programs for Muslim and non-Muslim community members to learn about topics essential to individual and community welfare.
theoryOfChange: The Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) has a published report titled "From the Roots of Trauma to the Flowering of Trauma-Informed Care." This document details TMWF's four-year project (starting in 2014) to design and implement culturally-sensitive trauma-informed programming for domestic violence survivors and their children. The report charts their process of becoming a trauma-informed agency, which involved learning about types of trauma and trauma-informed care, assessing and enhancing existing culturally-sensitive practices, training staff, and collaborating with researchers to document and build evidence-based practice. TMWF articulates a model that integrates culturally-sensitive services and trauma-informed advocacy, which they invite others to learn from and replicate.

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions that the score is tempered by moderate working capital reserves, but the working capital is at 3.7 months, which is below the common non-profit benchmark of 6-12 months for long-term stability. This should be described as low working capital, not moderate.
The risk summary mentions that the key concern is that the board is too small (2 members), but this is not mentioned in the amal_score_rationale. This should be included in the rationale.

impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that shelter and housing programs successfully transition families into permanent, safe environments.

strengths.detail: The charity spends only <cite id="10">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite>, significantly outperforming industry standards for lean operations.
TMWF holds <cite id="11">perfect 100/100 scores in Culture and Leadership</cite> from Charity Navigator, reflecting high organizational health.
In 2024, the foundation provided <cite id="12">emergency shelter to 137 residents</cite> and supported <cite id="13">216 non-residential domestic violence clients</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: Texas Muslim Women's Foundation excels in financial stewardship and transparency. The organization maintains <cite id="14">3.7 months of working capital</cite>, providing a resilient buffer for its shelter and housing programs. Their commitment to governance is evidenced by an <cite id="15">independent board with 100% membership</cite> meeting accountability standards. Furthermore, the charity has a <cite id="16">documented theory of change</cite>, ensuring that its social services and educational programs are strategically aligned to improve community well-being.
rich_narrative.summary: Founded in <cite id="1">2005</cite>, the Texas Muslim Women's Foundation (TMWF) provides a critical safety net for domestic violence survivors and families across North Texas. The organization operates with high financial efficiency, maintaining <cite id="2">a 98.67/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>. TMWF reported <cite id="3">$4,665,216 in total revenue</cite> for the 2023 fiscal year, though it faced a <cite id="4">19% revenue decline in 2024</cite>. Despite this fluctuation, the charity remains a primary provider of culturally sensitive social services, including <cite id="5">emergency shelter for 186 survivors</cite> and <cite id="6">transitional housing for 204 individuals</cite> in a single year. Their model focuses on direct service delivery, ensuring that <cite id="7">86.0% of expenses goes to programs</cite>. The foundation is particularly notable for its <cite id="8">zakat-eligible status</cite>, serving specific categories such as the poor (fuqara) and needy (masakin). By combining faith-based values with professional social work, TMWF addresses the unique needs of Muslim women while remaining <cite id="9">open to all community members</cite> regardless of background.
